sources.certVol1: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceAudit.note: Content is original/paraphrased from the cited sources; questions are original. Verify command syntax against official Cisco docs.

objectives.title: Configure and verify IPv4 addressing and subnetting

ckus.title: IPv4 Addressing
ckus.summary: A 32-bit address written as four dotted-decimal octets, paired with a subnet mask that splits it into a network portion and a host portion.

ckus.title: Subnet Mask
ckus.summary: A 32-bit value of contiguous 1s (network) then 0s (host). The prefix length (/n, CIDR) counts the 1s. Common masks: /24=255.255.255.0, /26=…192, /27=…224, /30=…252.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

ckus.title: Subnetting
ckus.summary: Borrowing host bits to create more, smaller networks. Hosts per subnet = 2^h − 2 (network + broadcast unusable); number of subnets = 2^(borrowed bits).

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

ckus.title: Block Size (Magic Number)
ckus.summary: Block size = 256 − (the interesting mask octet). Subnet boundaries are multiples of the block size; it gives the network address, broadcast, and usable range fast.

tiers.beginner: An IPv4 address has 32 bits, shown as four numbers 0–255 (like 192.168.1.10). A subnet mask decides which part is the “network” and which is the “host.” Subnetting just means splitting one network into several smaller ones by moving the dividing line. In every subnet, the first address (all host bits 0) is the network ID and the last (all host bits 1) is the broadcast — neither can be given to a device.
tiers.intermediate: The mask is 32 bits: 1s mark the network, 0s mark the host, written as /n (CIDR). Hosts per subnet = 2^h − 2 where h is the number of host bits (you subtract the network and broadcast addresses). To subnet, you borrow bits from the host side: borrowing b bits makes 2^b subnets, each smaller. The fastest way to find boundaries is the block size = 256 − the interesting mask octet; subnets start at multiples of that block size.
tiers.examReady: IPv4 = 32 bits / 4 octets + a mask of contiguous 1s (network) then 0s (host); the prefix /n is the count of 1s. Hosts/subnet = 2^h − 2 (h = host bits; network and broadcast are unusable). Subnets from borrowing b bits = 2^b. Block size (magic number) = 256 − interesting-octet mask value; subnet network IDs are multiples of the block size in that octet. For any address: network = host bits all 0, broadcast = host bits all 1, usable range = everything between. Examples: /26 (`255.255.255.192`) → block 64, 4 subnets (.0/.64/.128/.192), 62 hosts each; /30 (`255.255.255.252`) → block 4, 2 hosts (point-to-point links); /27 (`255.255.255.224`) → block 32, 30 hosts.

reading.definition: An IPv4 address is **32 bits** split by a **subnet mask** into network + host portions. **Subnetting** borrows host bits to make more, smaller networks; **block size** (`256 − mask octet`) locates subnet boundaries instantly.
keyPoints: Hosts per subnet = `2^h − 2` (h = host bits; network + broadcast are unusable).
Number of subnets when borrowing b bits = `2^b`.
Block size = `256 − interesting mask octet`; subnets start at multiples of it.
Network address = host bits all 0; broadcast = host bits all 1; usable = in between.
`/30` gives 2 usable hosts — ideal for point-to-point router links.
reading.realWorld: On a router, `ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0` sets an interface address + mask; `show ip interface brief` confirms it. Connected (`C`) and local (`L`, /32) routes appear automatically once the interface is up/up.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to subtract 2 for the network and broadcast addresses.
Assigning the network or broadcast address to a host.
Using the wrong octet for the block size on /8–/16 masks.
reading.advanced: VLSM (variable-length subnet masks) applies different masks to different subnets so each is sized to its host count, conserving addresses — e.g. /30 for WAN links and /26 for user LANs in the same network. Always allocate largest subnets first.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

questions.question: How many usable host addresses are in a `/26` subnet?
questions.explanation: /26 has 6 host bits → 2^6 − 2 = 62 usable hosts.

questions.question: What is the block size for a `/27` mask (`255.255.255.224`)?
questions.explanation: Block size = 256 − 224 = 32.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address of `192.168.10.0/26`?
questions.explanation: Block 64 → first subnet is .0–.63, so broadcast = .63.

questions.question: Which subnet does host `172.16.0.100/26` belong to?
questions.explanation: Block 64 → boundaries .0/.64/.128/.192; 100 falls in .64–.127, so the subnet is 172.16.0.64.

questions.question: Which mask provides at least 30 usable hosts with the fewest addresses wasted?
questions.explanation: /27 → 2^5 − 2 = 30 usable hosts exactly.

questions.question: How many usable hosts does a `/30` subnet provide?
questions.explanation: /30 → 2^2 − 2 = 2 usable hosts — perfect for a router-to-router link.

questions.question: Borrowing 3 host bits from a `/24`, how many subnets do you create?
questions.explanation: Number of subnets = 2^(borrowed bits) = 2^3 = 8.

questions.question: What is the CIDR prefix for the mask `255.255.255.240`?
questions.explanation: 240 = 11110000 → 4 bits in the last octet; 24 + 4 = /28.

questions.question: What is the usable host range of `10.0.0.0/29`?
questions.explanation: Block 8 → network .0, broadcast .7, usable .1–.6.

questions.question: How many host bits are in a `/26` address?
questions.explanation: 32 − 26 = 6 host bits.

questions.question: True or False: The network and broadcast addresses of a subnet can be assigned to hosts.
questions.explanation: False — both are reserved, which is why hosts = 2^h − 2.

questions.question: A LAN needs 200 hosts. What is the smallest standard mask that fits?
questions.explanation: /24 → 2^8 − 2 = 254 usable, the smallest mask that holds 200 hosts (/25 gives only 126).

flashcards.front: Formula for usable hosts per subnet?
flashcards.back: 2^h − 2, where h = host bits (subtract network + broadcast).

flashcards.front: Formula for number of subnets when borrowing b bits?
How do you find the block size (magic number)?
flashcards.back: 256 − the interesting mask octet. Subnets start at multiples of it.

flashcards.front: Network vs broadcast address of a subnet?
flashcards.back: Network = host bits all 0 (first); broadcast = host bits all 1 (last).
2 usable hosts — exactly right for a point-to-point router link.
The number of network (1) bits in the mask, counted from the left.

commands.purpose: Assign an IPv4 address and subnet mask to an interface.
List interfaces with their IP address and up/down status.
Show the connected/local routes created from interface addressing.

glossary.definition: 32-bit value of contiguous 1s (network) then 0s (host) that divides an IPv4 address.
The /n notation giving the number of network bits in the mask.
256 minus the interesting mask octet; the increment between subnet boundaries.
The first address of a subnet (host bits all 0); identifies the subnet, not a host.
The last address of a subnet (host bits all 1); reaches all hosts in the subnet.
Variable-Length Subnet Masking — using different masks per subnet to match each subnet’s size.

mnemonics.title: Block size
mnemonics.explanation: Subtract the interesting mask octet from 256 to get the block size; subnet IDs jump by that amount.

mnemonics.title: Powers of 2
mnemonics.explanation: Memorize the powers of 2 left-to-right; mask octet values are 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255.

examTraps.trap: Forgetting the “− 2” in the host formula.
examTraps.correction: Usable hosts = 2^h − 2 because the network and broadcast addresses can’t be assigned.
Borrowed bits give the number of subnets; remaining host bits give hosts per subnet.

examTraps.trap: Using the wrong octet for block size.
examTraps.correction: Apply block size to the octet where the mask is neither 255 nor 0 (the interesting octet).

examTraps.trap: Assuming /31 and /32 are normal host subnets.
examTraps.correction: /32 is a single host route; /31 is a special 2-address point-to-point (RFC 3021) with no broadcast.

misconceptions.misconception: A bigger prefix (/27) means a bigger network.
misconceptions.reality: A larger prefix number means MORE network bits and FEWER hosts — a smaller subnet.

misconceptions.misconception: You can assign the .0 or .255 address to a host.
misconceptions.reality: Within a subnet the all-0 host (network) and all-1 host (broadcast) are reserved.

misconceptions.misconception: The block size is always in the 4th octet.
misconceptions.reality: It applies to whichever octet the mask splits — e.g. a /18 subnets in the 3rd octet.

diagram.title: A /26 splits a /24 into four
annotations: Each subnet: 62 usable hosts (.1–.62 in the first).

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

steps.title: Find boundary
steps.action: Round the address’s octet down to the nearest multiple of the block size = network address.

steps.title: Broadcast
steps.action: Add block size − 1 to the network octet = broadcast address.

steps.title: Usable range
steps.action: Everything between network+1 and broadcast−1 is assignable.

ckus.title: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
ckus.summary: A connection-oriented Layer 4 protocol that provides reliability (acknowledgements + retransmission), sequencing, and flow control. IP protocol number 6.

ckus.title: UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
ckus.summary: A connectionless, best-effort Layer 4 protocol with no acknowledgements, sequencing, or flow control — only a checksum for error detection. Low overhead, low latency. IP protocol number 17.

ckus.title: Port Numbers
ckus.summary: Layer 4 addresses (0–65535) identifying the application/service. Well-known 0–1023, registered 1024–49151, ephemeral 49152–65535. A socket = IP + port.

ckus.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
ckus.summary: How TCP establishes a connection before data: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Connection teardown uses FIN/ACK exchanges.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

tiers.beginner: TCP and UDP are the two Layer 4 (transport) protocols. TCP is careful: it sets up a connection first, numbers the data, and makes sure everything arrives — re-sending anything lost. UDP just fires the data off without setup or guarantees (“best effort”). TCP is great for things that must be exact (web pages, files); UDP is great for things that must be fast (voice, video, DNS). Both use port numbers to label which application the data is for.
tiers.intermediate: TCP is connection-oriented: a three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) sets up the session before any data. It provides reliability (every segment is acknowledged; unacknowledged segments are retransmitted), sequencing (sequence numbers reorder out-of-order segments), and flow control (the window size lets the receiver throttle the sender). UDP is connectionless and best-effort — no handshake, no acknowledgements, no sequencing, no flow control — just a checksum for error detection, which makes it lighter and faster. Ports (0–65535) identify the application: well-known 0–1023, registered 1024–49151, ephemeral 49152–65535.
tiers.examReady: TCP = connection-oriented, reliable (ACK + retransmit), sequenced, flow-controlled (window size); IP protocol 6; three-way handshake SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. UDP = connectionless, best-effort, no ACK/sequencing/flow-control, checksum only; IP protocol 17; lower overhead → preferred for real-time voice/video and simple query/response (DNS, DHCP, TFTP, SNMP). Ports: well-known 0–1023, registered 1024–49151, ephemeral/dynamic 49152–65535. Know common ports: FTP `20/21`, SSH `22`, Telnet `23`, SMTP `25`, DNS `53`, DHCP `67/68`, TFTP `69`, HTTP `80`, HTTPS `443`, SNMP `161`, Syslog `514`, NTP `123`. DNS and some others use UDP by default but can fall back to TCP.

reading.definition: TCP and UDP are the two Layer 4 protocols. **TCP** is connection-oriented and **reliable** (handshake, acknowledgements, sequencing, flow control). **UDP** is connectionless and **best-effort** (no guarantees, just a checksum) — lighter and faster. Both use **port numbers** to identify the application.
keyPoints: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable, sequenced, flow-controlled. IP protocol `6`.
UDP: connectionless, best-effort, checksum only — low overhead. IP protocol `17`.
UDP preferred for real-time (voice/video) and simple query/response (DNS, DHCP, TFTP).
Reliability ≠ security — TCP is reliable but not encrypted.
reading.realWorld: A web download uses TCP `443` (HTTPS) so no bytes are lost; a VoIP call uses UDP because re-sending a late voice packet is useless — better to drop it. `show ip access-lists` and ACLs match on TCP/UDP port numbers to permit/deny specific apps.
commonMistakes: Saying UDP has “no error checking” — it has a checksum; it just has no recovery.
Thinking TCP reliability means TCP is secure (it is not — that is TLS).
Forgetting DNS uses UDP/53 by default (TCP/53 for large/zone transfers).
Confusing Layer 4 port numbers with physical switch ports.
reading.advanced: TCP flow control uses a sliding window; the receiver advertises a window size that grows/shrinks to match its buffer. TCP also does congestion control (slow start). The IPv4 header’s Protocol field (6 or 17) tells the receiver which Layer 4 header follows.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

questions.question: Which Layer 4 protocol is connection-oriented and reliable?
questions.explanation: TCP establishes a connection and guarantees delivery via acknowledgements and retransmission.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the TCP three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: The initiator sends SYN, the receiver replies SYN-ACK, the initiator confirms with ACK.

questions.question: Which protocol is connectionless and best-effort?
questions.explanation: UDP sends data without a connection or guarantees of delivery.

questions.question: What is the well-known port range?
questions.explanation: Well-known ports are 0–1023 (e.g. HTTP 80, HTTPS 443).

questions.question: A real-time VoIP call would most likely use which protocol, and why?
choices: UDP, for low latency and overhead
questions.explanation: Voice favors UDP — retransmitting late audio is useless, so low latency matters more than reliability.

questions.question: In the IPv4 header, which Protocol values indicate TCP and UDP?
questions.explanation: TCP = 6, UDP = 17 in the IPv4 Protocol field.

questions.question: Which TCP feature reorders segments that arrive out of order?
questions.explanation: Sequence numbers let the receiver reassemble segments in the right order.

questions.question: What port does HTTPS use?
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses TCP port 443; HTTP uses 80.

questions.question: Which TCP mechanism lets the receiver throttle the sender’s rate?
questions.explanation: The advertised window size implements flow control.

questions.question: True or False: UDP performs no error detection at all.
questions.explanation: False — UDP has a checksum for error detection; it just cannot recover (retransmit) lost data.

questions.question: Which statement about DNS transport is correct?
choices: DNS uses UDP/53 by default and TCP/53 for large responses/zone transfers
questions.explanation: DNS defaults to UDP/53 but falls back to TCP/53 for large messages and zone transfers.

questions.question: True or False: Because TCP is reliable, the data it carries is also encrypted/secure.
questions.explanation: False — reliability (delivery) and security (encryption) are different; TLS provides encryption, not TCP.

flashcards.front: Four services TCP provides that UDP does not?
flashcards.back: Connection setup (handshake), reliability (ACK + retransmit), sequencing, flow control.
Best-effort delivery + a checksum (error detection). No connection, ACK, sequencing, or flow control.

flashcards.front: IP protocol numbers for TCP and UDP?
Ports: SSH, Telnet, DNS, HTTP, HTTPS?
Ports: FTP, TFTP, DHCP, SNMP, Syslog?
When is UDP preferred over TCP?
flashcards.back: Real-time voice/video and simple query/response (DNS, DHCP, TFTP) — low latency/overhead.

commands.purpose: Display ACLs, which match on TCP/UDP protocol and port numbers.
On a host, list active TCP/UDP connections and listening ports.

commands.command: access-list 100 permit tcp any any eq 443
commands.purpose: Example extended-ACL line matching a TCP destination port (HTTPS).
commands.example: R1(config)# access-list 100 permit tcp any any eq 443

glossary.definition: Connection-oriented, reliable Layer 4 protocol (handshake, ACK, sequencing, flow control); IP protocol 6.
Connectionless, best-effort Layer 4 protocol with only a checksum; IP protocol 17.
TCP connection setup: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK.
A 0–65535 Layer 4 address identifying the application/service.
The combination of an IP address and a port number.
TCP’s use of window size to let the receiver pace the sender.

mnemonics.title: Handshake
mnemonics.explanation: Three steps to open a TCP connection — say it as a rhythm.

mnemonics.title: TCP vs UDP
mnemonics.mnemonic: TCP = Telephone Call (set up first), UDP = Unaddressed Drop-off (just send).
mnemonics.explanation: A call needs a connection and confirmation (TCP); a flyer dropped in a mailbox is best-effort (UDP).

examTraps.trap: Saying UDP has no error checking.
examTraps.correction: UDP has a checksum for error DETECTION; it just has no recovery/retransmission.
Reliable delivery ≠ encryption. TLS/IPsec provide security; TCP does not.
DNS uses UDP/53 by default, TCP/53 for large responses and zone transfers.

examTraps.trap: Mixing Layer 4 ports with switch ports.
examTraps.correction: Port numbers are Layer 4 application addresses, not physical interfaces.

misconceptions.misconception: UDP is “bad” because it is unreliable.
misconceptions.reality: UDP is ideal when speed beats guaranteed delivery — voice, video, DNS, DHCP all rely on it.
misconceptions.example: Re-sending a late voice packet would arrive too late to be useful.

misconceptions.misconception: TCP and UDP can use the same port for different apps without conflict.
misconceptions.reality: TCP/80 and UDP/80 are independent, but within one protocol a listening port maps to one service.
misconceptions.example: DNS listens on UDP/53 and TCP/53 separately.

misconceptions.reality: The SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK only establishes the connection; data flows after it completes.
misconceptions.example: A web page’s bytes start after the handshake finishes.

diagram.title: TCP three-way handshake
annotations: Connection is established only after all three messages.
Data transfer begins after the ACK.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

steps.title: SYN
steps.action: Client sends SYN with its initial sequence number.

steps.title: SYN-ACK
steps.action: Server replies SYN-ACK, acknowledging and sending its own sequence number.

steps.title: ACK
steps.action: Client ACKs the server’s sequence number; the connection is established.

steps.title: Data
steps.action: Application data flows reliably, acknowledged and sequenced.
